title: Binary Trees
description: Formalizing the two-children-per-node tree from Trees: the full/complete/perfect/balanced vocabulary, and counting nodes recursively.
objectives: Distinguish full, complete, perfect, and balanced binary trees
Build a small binary tree by hand
Count a binary tree's nodes recursively

sections.text: 1. Recap: We Already Used This Shape
Trees' TreeNode already had exactly left and right — that IS a binary tree. Now let's define it formally and learn its vocabulary.
A binary tree is a tree in which every node has at most two children, conventionally distinguished as left and right.

rows: Every node has either 0 or 2 children — never exactly 1
Every level is fully filled except possibly the last, which fills left to right (recall Heap's array-based tree — this is exactly that shape)
Every internal node has exactly 2 children, AND every leaf is at the same depth
The height of the two subtrees of any node never differs by more than some small bound (commonly 1) — kept roughly even

sections.text: These aren't mutually exclusive — a tree can be both full and balanced, for instance. "Balanced" specifically will matter a great deal once we reach Binary Search Trees, where an unbalanced tree can quietly ruin performance.

This builds the same shape used to compute height in Trees, extended with one more node — a small binary tree, five nodes, built entirely with new (recall Dynamic Memory Allocation) and pointer assignment.

This is the exact same recursive shape as height() from Trees — a base case for nullptr, and a recursive case combining both children's results. Most binary tree algorithms follow this identical template, just changing what gets combined and how.

sections.title: A single-elimination tournament bracket, always exactly two competitors per match
sections.text: A tournament bracket where every match has exactly two competitors (never one, never three) is a full binary tree in spirit — each match (node) either produces two child matches or is itself a final, leaf-level result. Counting how many total matches occurred is exactly countNodes(): count this match, plus everything in the left half of the bracket, plus everything in the right half.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Confusing complete and perfect binary trees — complete only requires the last level to fill left-to-right; perfect requires every leaf at the exact same depth.
Forgetting the base case (node == nullptr) in a recursive tree function — without it, the recursion never terminates.
Assuming a binary tree is automatically balanced — balance is a separate property that must be actively maintained or checked, not guaranteed just by being binary.
Writing a loop-based node counter when the recursive version is both simpler and more directly matches the tree's own recursive structure.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a binary tree?
questions.answer: A tree in which every node has at most two children, conventionally called left and right.

questions.question: What is the difference between a complete and a perfect binary tree?
questions.answer: A complete binary tree fills every level fully except possibly the last, which fills left to right; a perfect binary tree requires every internal node to have exactly two children and every leaf to be at the same depth.

questions.question: What does 'balanced' mean for a binary tree?
questions.answer: The height of the two subtrees of any node never differs by more than a small bound (commonly 1), keeping the tree roughly even rather than lopsided.

questions.question: How would you count the total number of nodes in a binary tree?
questions.answer: Recursively: a nullptr node contributes 0, and any real node contributes 1 plus the node counts of its left and right subtrees.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A binary tree is a tree where every node has at most two children, left and right. Several named shapes matter: full (every node has 0 or 2 children, never 1), complete (every level full except possibly the last, filled left to right — the shape Heap relies on), perfect (every leaf at the same depth), and balanced (subtree heights never differ by more than a small bound), which becomes especially important once we reach Binary Search Trees. Counting a binary tree's nodes follows the same recursive template as computing its height: a nullptr contributes 0, and any real node contributes 1 plus the counts of its left and right subtrees.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Binary Trees
sections.text: Binary tree = a tournament bracket, always 0 or 2 per match. Full = never exactly 1 child. Complete = last row fills left to right. Perfect = every leaf same depth. Balanced = neither side towers over the other.

sections.title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth (the number of nodes along the longest path from root to a leaf).
hints: This is the exact same recursive shape as this lesson's countNodes() — a nullptr contributes a base value, and a real node combines its children's results.
The depth of a node is 1 (itself) plus the LARGER of its two subtrees' depths, not the sum.
Base case: an empty tree (nullptr) has depth 0.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Level-order BFS, counting how many full levels are processed.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A short recursive definition: 1 + the larger of the two children's depths.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) call stack, h = tree height

sections.title: Same Tree
sections.problemStatement: Given the roots of two binary trees p and q, return true if they are structurally identical with the same node values.
hints: Two trees match only if BOTH are empty at the same spots, or BOTH have a node with the same value AND matching left/right subtrees — a direct recursive comparison.
Base cases: both nullptr → true (matching empty). Exactly one nullptr → false (structural mismatch).
Recursive case: values must match, AND both left subtrees must match, AND both right subtrees must match.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Serialize both trees to strings (preorder with null markers) and compare the strings.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Direct recursive comparison, no serialization needed.
